title: Accessibility & Quality
description: TalkBack, Compose semantics, touch targets, Android Vitals, staged rollouts, and release management
lesson:
Accessibility is Not Optional

15% of the world's population has some form of disability. Accessibility is a legal requirement in many markets and a Google Play quality signal. Senior engineers build it in from the start.

Why it matters beyond compliance: Accessible apps are better apps for everyone. Good contentDescription helps voice control users. Proper touch targets help users with large fingers or in moving vehicles. High contrast helps in bright sunlight. Semantic structure helps automated testing tools find and interact with your UI. Accessibility is quality.

TalkBack Fundamentals: How It Works

TalkBack is Android's built-in screen reader. Understanding how it works under the hood helps you build for it correctly.

The AccessibilityNodeInfo Tree

Every View (or Compose semantics node) is represented as an AccessibilityNodeInfo in a parallel tree that mirrors your UI hierarchy. TalkBack doesn't see your Views directly — it sees this accessibility tree.

Your UI Hierarchy Accessibility Tree (what TalkBack sees) ───────────────── ───────────────────────────────────────── LinearLayout AccessibilityNodeInfo (root) ├─ ImageView ├─ Node: "Profile photo" (IMAGE) ├─ TextView ├─ Node: "John Smith" (TEXT) └─ Button └─ Node: "Send message" (BUTTON, clickable) TalkBack navigates the RIGHT side. It never sees your LinearLayout, ImageView, or Button classes — only the AccessibilityNodeInfo data.

Each AccessibilityNodeInfo contains:

PropertyWhat TalkBack Does With ItCompose Equivalent
contentDescriptionReads it aloud as the element's labelsemantics { contentDescription = "..." }
textReads it aloud (for TextViews, EditTexts)Automatic for Text()
classNameAnnounces type: "Button", "Checkbox", etc.semantics { role = Role.Button }
isChecked / isSelectedAnnounces state: "checked", "selected"semantics { toggleableState = ... }
isClickableAnnounces "double-tap to activate"Automatic with clickable { }
isHeadingAllows jump-by-heading navigationsemantics { heading() }
liveRegionAuto-announces content changessemantics { liveRegion = LiveRegionMode.Polite }

What Gets Read and In What Order

TalkBack reads elements in accessibility traversal order, which by default follows the visual layout: left-to-right, top-to-bottom. When the user swipe-right, TalkBack moves to the next node. TalkBack announces a combination of:

// TalkBack announcement pattern: // [contentDescription OR text] + [role type] + [state] + [action hint] // Example: A checked checkbox with text // TalkBack reads: "Enable notifications. Checkbox. Checked. Double-tap to toggle." // Example: A button // TalkBack reads: "Submit order. Button. Double-tap to activate." // Example: An image with contentDescription // TalkBack reads: "User profile photo. Image." // Example: An image with contentDescription = null // TalkBack SKIPS this element entirely — it's treated as decorative

contentDescription Best Practices

// GOOD: Describes what the image MEANS, not what it LOOKS LIKE Image( painter = painterResource(R.drawable.error_icon), contentDescription = "Error" // Not "Red circle with X" ) // GOOD: Includes relevant state information Icon( imageVector = if (isFavorite) Icons.Filled.Star else Icons.Outlined.Star, contentDescription = if (isFavorite) "Remove from favorites" else "Add to favorites" ) // BAD: Don't include the element type — TalkBack adds it Image( painter = painterResource(R.drawable.send), contentDescription = "Send button" // TalkBack would say "Send button. Button." — redundant! ) // BAD: Don't say "image of" or "picture of" Image( painter = painterResource(R.drawable.profile), contentDescription = "Image of John" // TalkBack says "Image of John. Image." — redundant! ) // GOOD: Just describe the content Image( painter = painterResource(R.drawable.profile), contentDescription = "John Smith's profile photo" )
Never leave contentDescription empty string (""). Empty string means "I explicitly set this to nothing" — TalkBack still focuses the element but reads nothing, confusing users. Use null for decorative elements (TalkBack skips them entirely) or provide a meaningful description.

Compose Semantics — The Full Picture

In Jetpack Compose, the semantics tree IS the accessibility tree. Every composable can contribute semantic information via the semantics { } modifier. Compose automatically populates many semantic properties from built-in composables (Text, Button, Checkbox, etc.), but custom composables need manual semantics.

Basic Semantics

// Basic content description Image( painter = painterResource(R.drawable.profile), contentDescription = "User profile photo" // Read by TalkBack ) // Decorative image — skip for screen readers Image( painter = painterResource(R.drawable.divider), contentDescription = null // TalkBack ignores this ) // Merge children into single TalkBack element Row(modifier = Modifier.semantics(mergeDescendants = true) {}) { Icon(Icons.Default.Star, contentDescription = null) Text("4.5 stars") } // TalkBack reads: "4.5 stars" (one element, not two)

Role — What Type of Element Is This?

Role tells TalkBack what kind of control this is, so it can announce the right type and available actions:

// Available Roles: Role.Button // "Button. Double-tap to activate." Role.Checkbox // "Checkbox. Checked/Not checked. Double-tap to toggle." Role.Switch // "Switch. On/Off. Double-tap to toggle." Role.RadioButton // "Radio button. Selected/Not selected." Role.Tab // "Tab. Selected/Not selected." Role.Image // "Image." Role.DropdownList // "Drop-down list." // Applying a role to a custom composable Box(modifier = Modifier .clickable { onClick() } .semantics { role = Role.Button } ) { Text("Submit") } // Without role: TalkBack just says "Submit. Double-tap to activate." // With role: TalkBack says "Submit. Button. Double-tap to activate."

stateDescription — Custom State Announcements

// stateDescription overrides the default state announcement // Useful for sliders, progress, and custom controls // Custom progress indicator Box( modifier = Modifier.semantics { stateDescription = "$progress% complete" progressBarRangeInfo = ProgressBarRangeInfo( current = progress / 100f, range = 0f..1f ) } ) { CustomProgressBar(progress) } // Custom toggle with descriptive states Box( modifier = Modifier .toggleable(value = isDarkMode, onValueChange = { toggle() }) .semantics { stateDescription = if (isDarkMode) "Dark mode" else "Light mode" } ) { Text("Theme") } // TalkBack reads: "Theme. Switch. Dark mode. Double-tap to toggle."

heading() — Section Navigation

// Mark text as a heading for TalkBack heading navigation // Users can swipe up/down to jump between headings — like H1/H2 on the web Text( text = "Account Settings", style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineMedium, modifier = Modifier.semantics { heading() } ) // Headings create a navigation structure: // "General" (heading) // Language selector // Theme toggle // "Privacy" (heading) // Location permission // Data sharing toggle // // User swipes down between headings → jumps from "General" to "Privacy" // Much faster than swiping through every element!

clearAndSetSemantics — Full Override

// Override all children's semantics with a single description Box(modifier = Modifier.clearAndSetSemantics { contentDescription = "Rating: 4.5 out of 5 stars" }) { StarRating(rating = 4.5f) // Children's semantics are cleared } // When to use clearAndSetSemantics: // 1. Custom-drawn components (Canvas, custom painting) // 2. Complex composite elements where child semantics would be confusing // 3. When the sum of children's descriptions doesn't convey the meaning // Example: Price display with multiple styled text elements Row(modifier = Modifier.clearAndSetSemantics { contentDescription = "Price: 29 dollars and 99 cents" }) { Text("$", style = smallStyle) Text("29", style = largeStyle) Text(".99", style = smallStyle) } // Without clear: TalkBack reads "Dollar sign. 29. .99" — confusing // With clear: TalkBack reads "Price: 29 dollars and 99 cents" — clear!

Custom Accessibility Actions

For elements with multiple actions (like a swipeable list item with delete and archive), custom actions let TalkBack users access all functionality:

// Custom actions appear in TalkBack's action menu (swipe up/down to cycle) Row( modifier = Modifier .clickable { onItemClick(email.id) } .semantics { customActions = listOf( CustomAccessibilityAction("Delete") { onDelete(email.id) true // return true if action was handled }, CustomAccessibilityAction("Archive") { onArchive(email.id) true }, CustomAccessibilityAction("Mark as read") { onMarkRead(email.id) true } ) } ) { EmailItemContent(email) } // TalkBack users: swipe down on item → "Delete", swipe down → "Archive", etc. // This replaces the swipe-to-delete gesture that TalkBack users can't perform
Why custom actions matter: Swipe gestures (swipe-to-delete, swipe-to-archive) are consumed by TalkBack for navigation. Custom accessibility actions provide an alternative way for screen reader users to access the same functionality.

Touch Target Sizes

Minimum touch target size: 48dp x 48dp (Material Design / WCAG). This isn't about visual size — a 24dp icon can have a 48dp touch area. Small targets cause frustration for all users, especially those with motor impairments.

// Material components handle this automatically IconButton(onClick = { ... }) { // Already 48dp minimum Icon(Icons.Default.Close, "Close", modifier = Modifier.size(24.dp)) } // For custom components — use minimumInteractiveComponentSize() // This is the Compose-idiomatic way to ensure touch targets Icon( imageVector = Icons.Default.Info, contentDescription = "More info", modifier = Modifier .size(16.dp) // Visual size: tiny 16dp .minimumInteractiveComponentSize() // Touch target: 48dp! .clickable { showInfo() } ) // Manual approach — explicit minimum size constraint Modifier.sizeIn(minWidth = 48.dp, minHeight = 48.dp) // For composables that already define their own padding, // measure whether the total clickable area meets 48dp Box( modifier = Modifier .defaultMinSize(minWidth = 48.dp, minHeight = 48.dp) .clickable { onTap() }, contentAlignment = Alignment.Center ) { Icon(Icons.Default.Add, "Add", modifier = Modifier.size(20.dp)) }
Common mistake: Adding clickable to a small icon without expanding its touch area. The icon may be visually 24dp but the touch target is also 24dp — half the minimum. Use IconButton or minimumInteractiveComponentSize() to fix this.

Streaming Text and Accessibility — Live Regions

When text changes dynamically (error messages, status updates, AI response streaming), TalkBack users won't know unless you tell TalkBack to announce the changes. This is what live regions do.

Polite vs Assertive

ModeBehaviorUse Case
LiveRegionMode.PoliteWaits for TalkBack to finish current speech, then announcesStatus updates, streaming AI responses, progress indicators, chat messages
LiveRegionMode.AssertiveInterrupts current speech immediatelyCritical errors, urgent alerts, timer expiration

AI Response Streaming — The Right Pattern

When streaming AI responses (like a ChatGPT-style interface), text arrives in chunks. Without live regions, TalkBack users see nothing until they manually navigate to the text.

// CORRECT: Use Polite live region for streaming AI text @Composable fun AiResponseBubble(responseText: String, isStreaming: Boolean) { Text( text = responseText, modifier = Modifier.semantics { // Polite: TalkBack announces new content without interrupting // As new chunks arrive and responseText grows, TalkBack reads the additions liveRegion = LiveRegionMode.Polite } ) } // WHY Polite and not Assertive? // - Polite waits for current speech → natural reading flow // - Assertive would interrupt EVERY chunk → chaotic, garbled speech // - AI streaming sends many small updates → Polite batches them naturally // CAUTION: TalkBack announces EVERY text change in a live region // If you update text 60 times/second, you'll flood TalkBack // Debounce or batch updates for accessibility: val debouncedText by remember(responseText) { derivedStateOf { // Only update TalkBack when a sentence boundary is reached responseText } } // Use Assertive ONLY for critical interruptions: Text( text = "Connection lost. Reconnecting...", modifier = Modifier.semantics { liveRegion = LiveRegionMode.Assertive // Interrupt immediately! } )

Error Messages and Snackbars

// Error message that appears dynamically if (error != null) { Text( text = error, color = MaterialTheme.colorScheme.error, modifier = Modifier.semantics { liveRegion = LiveRegionMode.Polite // Announce when error appears } ) } // Snackbar content — Material Snackbar handles this automatically // But custom snackbar implementations need manual live regions

Color Contrast

WCAG AA requires minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18sp+ or 14sp+ bold). WCAG AAA requires 7:1.

LevelNormal TextLarge Text (18sp+ or 14sp+ bold)UI Components & Graphics
WCAG AA (required)4.5:13:13:1
WCAG AAA (enhanced)7:14.5:1N/A
// Examples of contrast ratios: // Black (#000000) on White (#FFFFFF) = 21:1 ✓ Exceeds AAA // Dark Gray (#595959) on White (#FFFFFF) = 7:1 ✓ Meets AAA // Gray (#767676) on White (#FFFFFF) = 4.5:1 ✓ Meets AA // Light Gray (#949494) on White (#FFFFFF) = 2.9:1 ✗ FAILS AA! // Common mistake: disabled state text is too low contrast // Material Design uses 38% opacity for disabled → may fail AA // Solution: test with Accessibility Scanner and adjust if needed
Testing tools for contrast:
Accessibility Scanner — Google's free app, run it over your screen to detect contrast failures
Layout Inspector — Inspect Compose semantics tree and validate element properties
WebAIM Contrast Checker — Online tool for checking specific color pairs
Android Studio lint — TextContrastCheck lint rule flags low-contrast text in XML layouts

Screen Reader Testing: How to Test with TalkBack

You cannot ship accessible apps without testing with TalkBack. Here is how to do it effectively:

Enabling TalkBack

// On device: Settings → Accessibility → TalkBack → Turn on // Shortcut: Hold both volume keys for 3 seconds (if enabled) // On emulator via ADB: adb shell settings put secure enabled_accessibility_services \ com.google.android.marvin.talkback/com.google.android.marvin.talkback.TalkBackService adb shell settings put secure accessibility_enabled 1 // Disable TalkBack via ADB (useful when stuck!): adb shell settings put secure enabled_accessibility_services "" adb shell settings put secure accessibility_enabled 0

TalkBack Navigation Gestures

GestureAction
Swipe rightMove to next element
Swipe leftMove to previous element
Double-tapActivate (click) the focused element
Swipe up then downOpen TalkBack context menu (actions)
Swipe down then upChange navigation granularity (characters, words, headings, links)
Swipe downNext item within granularity (next heading, next link, etc.)
Swipe upPrevious item within granularity
Two-finger swipe upScroll down
Two-finger swipe downScroll up

Common Issues Found During TalkBack Testing

TalkBack Testing Checklist: ✗ Unlabeled buttons → Icon with no contentDescription Fix: Add meaningful contentDescription ✗ "Button 1", "Button 2" → Auto-generated labels Fix: Provide descriptive contentDescription ✗ Element reads child text separately → Missing mergeDescendants Fix: semantics(mergeDescendants = true) on the container ✗ Decorative images read aloud → contentDescription should be null Fix: Set contentDescription = null for decorative elements ✗ Dynamic content not announced → Missing live region Fix: Add liveRegion = LiveRegionMode.Polite ✗ Custom controls not described → Missing role and stateDescription Fix: Add semantics { role = Role.Switch; stateDescription = "On" } ✗ Can't perform swipe action → Gesture consumed by TalkBack Fix: Add custom accessibility actions as alternative

Accessibility Tree in Layout Inspector

Android Studio's Layout Inspector shows the Compose semantics tree alongside the visual layout. This is the same tree that TalkBack navigates.

Layout Inspector → Semantics Tab: Composable Semantics Properties ───────────── ────────────────────────────────────── ChatScreen (no semantics — layout only) TopAppBar mergeDescendants = true Text text = "Chat", heading IconButton role = Button Icon contentDescription = "Settings" LazyColumn MessageItem mergeDescendants = true, clickable Text text = "Hello there!" Text text = "2:45 PM" MessageItem mergeDescendants = true, clickable Text text = "How are you?" Text text = "2:46 PM" TextField role = TextField, editableText Button role = Button, text = "Send"
Use the Layout Inspector to verify: (1) Are elements merged correctly? (2) Do custom components have the right role? (3) Are headings marked? (4) Is the traversal order logical? (5) Are live regions set on dynamic content?

AccessibilityManager — Screen Data Handoff to LLMs

Before computer control and screenshot analysis existed, AccessibilityManager and accessibility services were the primary way to programmatically read what's on screen — and this pattern was used to hand off screen data to LLMs for understanding and interaction.

// AccessibilityService can read the entire screen's accessibility tree class ScreenReaderService : AccessibilityService() { override fun onAccessibilityEvent(event: AccessibilityEvent) { // Get the root node of the current screen val rootNode: AccessibilityNodeInfo? = rootInActiveWindow // Walk the tree to extract all visible text and structure val screenData = buildString { traverseTree(rootNode, depth = 0) } // This structured text could be sent to an LLM: // "Screen: Settings // Heading: General // Switch: Dark Mode [ON] // Switch: Notifications [OFF] // Heading: Privacy // Button: Clear Data" } private fun StringBuilder.traverseTree( node: AccessibilityNodeInfo?, depth: Int ) { node ?: return val indent = " ".repeat(depth) val text = node.text ?: node.contentDescription ?: "" val type = node.className?.toString()?.substringAfterLast('.') ?: "" if (text.isNotEmpty()) { appendLine("$indent$type: $text") } for (i in 0 until node.childCount) { traverseTree(node.getChild(i), depth + 1) } } override fun onInterrupt() {} }
Historical context: Before vision models could analyze screenshots directly, accessibility services were the only way to extract structured screen content for AI processing. Apps like Android's Switch Access and third-party automation tools used this approach. Modern computer control (like Anthropic's computer use) now uses screenshots + vision models, but the accessibility tree approach is still useful for: (1) faster than sending images, (2) works with screen-off displays, (3) provides semantic structure (roles, states, actions) that vision models must infer.

Android Vitals

Google Play Console tracks app quality via Android Vitals. Bad metrics reduce your app's visibility:

MetricBad ThresholdMeaning
Crash rate> 1.09%User-perceived crash sessions
ANR rate> 0.47%App Not Responding (main thread blocked 5s+)
Excessive wakeups> 10/hourAlarmManager waking device too often
Stuck wake locks> 0.10%Wake locks held for 1+ hour

Release Management

  • Internal testing — Team only, instant publish, no review
  • Closed testing — Invited users, some review
  • Open testing — Anyone can join, full review
  • Production — Staged rollout: 1% → 5% → 10% → 25% → 50% → 100%
Pre-launch reports: Google Play automatically runs your app on Firebase Test Lab devices during review, catching crashes and accessibility issues before users see them.

questions.title: Minimum Touch Target
questions.description: What is the minimum recommended touch target size for interactive elements?
options: 24dp x 24dp
36dp x 36dp
48dp x 48dp
64dp x 64dp
questions.explanation: Material Design and WCAG guidelines require a minimum 48dp x 48dp touch target. This ensures users with motor impairments can reliably tap elements. Note: the visual size can be smaller (e.g., a 24dp icon), but the touchable area must be at least 48dp. Material components like IconButton handle this automatically.
hints: This is a Material Design fundamental

questions.title: Compose Semantics
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to make a custom rating component accessible to TalkBack.

blanks.hint: Clears children's semantics and sets custom ones
The text TalkBack will read aloud

questions.explanation: clearAndSetSemantics replaces all children's semantic data with a single, clear description. Without it, TalkBack would try to read the Canvas — which has no meaningful semantics. This is essential for custom-drawn components like star ratings, charts, or progress indicators.
hints: Canvas elements have no inherent accessibility — you must provide it
You want ONE description for the whole component, not per-star

questions.title: mergeDescendants
questions.description: How does TalkBack treat this composable?
options: TalkBack reads each element separately: image, title, subtitle — 3 swipes to navigate
TalkBack reads all text as one element: "[title], [subtitle]" — 1 swipe, image is skipped because contentDescription is null
TalkBack skips the entire Row because mergeDescendants hides it
TalkBack only reads the first child element
questions.explanation: mergeDescendants = true combines all children into a single TalkBack element. The Image has contentDescription = null so it's skipped. TalkBack reads "[title], [subtitle]" as one unit with one swipe. The clickable modifier makes the entire Row tappable. This is the correct pattern for list items — users shouldn't have to swipe through each sub-element.
hints: List items should be one TalkBack element, not many
contentDescription = null means 'decorative, skip this'

questions.title: Color Contrast Ratio
questions.description: What is the minimum color contrast ratio required by WCAG AA for normal-sized text?
options: 3:1 (large text only)
7:1 (that's WCAG AAA)
questions.explanation: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18sp+ regular or 14sp+ bold). WCAG AAA requires 7:1. Low contrast makes text unreadable for users with low vision — and difficult for everyone in bright sunlight. Test with Android's Accessibility Scanner.
hints: AA is the standard level, AAA is the enhanced level

questions.title: Android Vitals Thresholds
questions.description: Above which crash rate does Google Play flag your app as having bad behavior in Android Vitals?
options: 0.1% — essentially crash-free
0.47% — same as ANR threshold
1.09% — user-perceived crash rate
5% — only very crashy apps
questions.explanation: Google Play flags apps with a user-perceived crash rate above 1.09% and ANR rate above 0.47%. Exceeding these thresholds can reduce your app's discoverability in the Play Store and show warnings to users. Monitor via Play Console → Android Vitals.
hints: ANR has a stricter threshold than crashes

questions.title: Missing Accessibility
questions.description: TalkBack users can't interact with this custom toggle. Fix it so TalkBack announces the state and toggle action.
questions.explanation: Custom toggles need: (1) Role.Switch so TalkBack announces "Switch" instead of "Button", (2) toggleable instead of clickable to convey on/off semantics, and (3) stateDescription so TalkBack says "On" or "Off". Without these, TalkBack users have no idea what the element does or its current state.
hints: TalkBack needs to know: what is this? (Role) and what state is it in? (stateDescription)
Use toggleable instead of clickable for on/off controls

questions.title: LiveRegion
questions.description: When should you use liveRegion = LiveRegionMode.Polite in Compose semantics?
options: For all Text composables to ensure TalkBack reads them
For dynamically changing content (error messages, status updates) that should be announced without the user navigating to it
For interactive elements like buttons and text fields
For images that change based on state
questions.explanation: LiveRegion.Polite tells TalkBack to announce content changes without interrupting current speech. Use it for: error messages that appear, status updates ("Upload complete"), timer displays, and snackbar content. LiveRegionMode.Assertive interrupts immediately — use only for critical alerts. Don't overuse either, or TalkBack becomes noisy.
hints: The key is 'dynamic content that changes without user navigation'
Polite waits for current speech to finish, Assertive interrupts

questions.title: Staged Rollout Strategy
questions.description: You've released v2.0 to 5% of users and Android Vitals shows the crash rate jumped to 2.5%. What should you do?
options: Continue the rollout — 5% is too small to be meaningful
Halt the rollout immediately, investigate the crash, fix it, and either resume with a patch or roll back to v1.x
Increase to 50% to get more data on the crash
Wait 48 hours to see if the crash rate stabilizes
questions.explanation: A 2.5% crash rate is well above the 1.09% bad behavior threshold. Halt immediately — Google Play allows you to pause a staged rollout, keeping existing users on v2.0 but stopping new ones from getting it. Investigate the crash, ship a fix as v2.0.1, and resume the rollout. If the crash is severe, roll back to v1.x entirely. Never expand a rollout with a known high crash rate.
hints: 1.09% is the bad behavior threshold — 2.5% is way over
Staged rollouts exist precisely for this scenario

questions.title: Custom Accessibility Actions
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to add swipe-to-delete and swipe-to-archive as accessibility actions on a list item, so TalkBack users can access these gestures.

blanks.hint: The semantics property that holds a list of extra actions
The class that creates a named action with a lambda handler

questions.explanation: customActions is the semantics property that holds additional actions beyond the default click. Each CustomAccessibilityAction takes a label string (read by TalkBack) and a lambda that returns true if the action was handled. TalkBack users cycle through these actions by swiping up/down on the focused element. This is critical for any list item that uses swipe gestures, because TalkBack consumes swipe gestures for navigation.
hints: Swipe gestures are not available to TalkBack users — they need an alternative
The actions appear in TalkBack's local context menu on the focused element

questions.title: Live Region for AI Streaming
questions.description: How does TalkBack handle this composable as the AI response streams in word-by-word?
options: TalkBack reads the full text once when streaming completes — no intermediate announcements
TalkBack ignores the text changes because it only reads on user navigation
TalkBack interrupts itself on EVERY text change, producing garbled speech like "The— The ans— The answer— The answer is— The answer is 42"
TalkBack smoothly reads each new word as it arrives, waiting politely between updates
questions.explanation: LiveRegionMode.Assertive interrupts current speech immediately on every text change. With rapid streaming updates, TalkBack will try to announce each change but immediately interrupt itself with the next one — producing garbled, unusable output. The fix is to use LiveRegionMode.Polite instead, which waits for current speech to finish. Even better: debounce updates so TalkBack only sees changes at sentence boundaries, not every word.
hints: Assertive means 'interrupt immediately' — what happens when you interrupt 10 times per second?
Consider the difference between Polite (waits) and Assertive (interrupts)

questions.title: AccessibilityNodeInfo Tree
questions.description: When TalkBack navigates your app, what does it actually interact with?
options: The View hierarchy directly — TalkBack reads View classes like Button, TextView, and ImageView
The Compose recomposition tree — TalkBack triggers recomposition to get updated text
The AccessibilityNodeInfo tree — a parallel tree of accessibility data that mirrors your UI hierarchy
The Window Manager surface — TalkBack reads pixels from the display buffer
questions.explanation: TalkBack interacts with the AccessibilityNodeInfo tree, not your Views or Composables directly. Each visible UI element has a corresponding AccessibilityNodeInfo that contains: contentDescription, text, className (role), state (checked, selected), and available actions (click, scroll). In Compose, the semantics tree maps directly to AccessibilityNodeInfo nodes. This separation means you control what TalkBack sees by setting semantic properties — the visual appearance and the accessibility representation are independent.
hints: TalkBack is a separate service — it doesn't have direct access to your app's View objects
Think about what data structure would allow a screen reader to work across all apps

questions.title: Inaccessible Price Display
questions.description: TalkBack reads this price as three separate elements: "Dollar sign", "29", ".99" — confusing for users. Fix it so TalkBack reads a single, clear price announcement.

checks.message: Use clearAndSetSemantics or mergeDescendants to combine the price into one accessible element

questions.explanation: clearAndSetSemantics replaces all children's semantic data with a single, clear description. Without it, TalkBack reads each Text composable separately — "Dollar sign. 29. .99" — which is confusing and unprofessional. With it, TalkBack reads "29 dollars and 99 cents" — clear and unambiguous. An alternative is semantics(mergeDescendants = true), which would merge the text into "$29.99", but clearAndSetSemantics gives you full control over the announcement wording.
hints: The three Text elements need to be combined into one accessible element
You want TalkBack to read a human-friendly price, not the raw characters
